_note: Offline content library for Second Life AI. One entry per model class. Bin advice is a common municipal scheme and varies by locality; the site says so.

cardboard.name: Cardboard
cardboard.tagline: Corrugated boxes, shipping cartons, cereal boxes.
cardboard.material: Kraft paper fiber, often layered with a corrugated middle for strength.
cardboard.recyclable: Yes — one of the most recycled materials on earth.
cardboard.decompose_landfill: 2 months in the open, but far longer sealed in a dry landfill.
prep: Empty it and knock off any food or grease
Flatten it so it takes less space
Keep it dry — wet cardboard is harder to recycle
Remove large tape and plastic labels if you can
second_life: Molded pulp egg cartons and trays
cardboard.future_good: Flattened and kept dry, this box is baled, pulped, and pressed back into fresh board within weeks. It may be holding someone else's delivery before the month is out.
cardboard.future_bad: Tossed in general waste, it's buried where no oxygen reaches it. Sealed in the dark, it can sit intact for decades instead of becoming a new box in a matter of weeks.
cardboard.rewrite: You pulled it back from the landfill. This fiber has already been a tree, a box, and a box again — you just gave it one more life.
cardboard.did_you_know: Cardboard fiber can be recycled 5 to 7 times before it gets too short to bond.

glass.name: Glass
glass.tagline: Bottles and jars — clear, green, or brown.
glass.material: Silica sand, soda ash, and limestone melted together. Endlessly reformable.
glass.recyclable: Yes — and infinitely, with no loss of quality.
glass.decompose_landfill: It effectively never breaks down — estimates run to a million years.
prep: Rinse out food and drink residue
Leave labels on — they burn off in melting
Keep lids separate (they're usually metal or plastic)
Don't include drinking glasses, mirrors, or ceramics — different melting points
second_life: Aggregate in road surfaces and tiles
glass.future_good: Rinsed and sorted by color, this jar is crushed into 'cullet,' melted, and blown into a new container. Recycled glass melts at a lower temperature, so every jar saved cuts the furnace's energy bill.
glass.future_bad: In landfill it simply waits. Glass doesn't rot, rust, or fade — a bottle buried today could outlast every building standing above it.
glass.rewrite: Glass is the closest thing to immortal in your bin. You just kept this one in the loop — it can become a new jar again and again, forever.
glass.did_you_know: A recycled glass bottle can go from your bin back onto a shelf in as little as 30 days.

metal.name: Metal
metal.tagline: Aluminum cans, steel tins, foil, bottle caps.
metal.material: Mostly aluminum or steel — both recyclable without limit.
metal.recyclable: Yes — and recycling aluminum saves about 95% of the energy of making it new.
metal.decompose_landfill: Aluminum: 80–200 years. Steel: up to 50 years to corrode.
prep: Rinse out food and drink
You can leave labels on cans
Squash cans to save space (optional)
Collect small foil into a ball so it doesn't fall through the sorter
second_life: New cans — often back on a shelf within weeks
metal.future_good: Rinsed and recycled, this can is shredded, melted, and rolled into new sheet metal. An aluminum can is the fastest recycler in your bin — it can be back on the shelf in about 60 days.
metal.future_bad: Buried instead, it slowly corrodes over a century or two, and all the energy locked into refining that metal from ore is lost for good.
metal.rewrite: Making this can from raw ore took twenty times the energy of recycling it. You just saved the other nineteen.
metal.did_you_know: Recycling one aluminum can saves enough energy to run a laptop for several hours.

paper.name: Paper
paper.material: Plant fiber, usually wood pulp — sometimes recycled fiber already.
paper.recyclable: Yes, if it's clean and dry.
paper.decompose_landfill: 2–6 weeks if exposed, but it can persist for years packed in a dry landfill.
prep: Keep it clean and dry
Remove plastic windows from envelopes if you can
Greasy or food-soaked paper goes to general waste, not recycling
Staples are fine — they're removed at the mill
paper.future_good: Clean and dry, this sheet is pulped, screened, and rolled into new paper within days. Recycled paper uses far less water and energy than starting from a fresh-cut tree.
paper.future_bad: Soaked or thrown in the trash, its fibers are lost. In a dry landfill even paper can stay readable for years, doing nothing, when it could have been a new page.
paper.rewrite: Every sheet you recycle is a fraction of a tree left standing. This one gets to be a page again instead of ending in the ground.
paper.did_you_know: A tonne of recycled paper spares around 17 trees and thousands of liters of water.

plastic.name: Plastic
plastic.material: Petroleum-based polymers. Types vary a lot — check the number in the triangle.
plastic.recyclable: Sometimes — bottles (#1, #2) recycle well; films and mixed plastics often don't.
plastic.decompose_landfill: It doesn't biodegrade — it breaks into microplastics over 400+ years.
prep: Rinse out food and drink
Empty and squash bottles, then re-cap them
Check for a recycling number — #1 and #2 are widely accepted
Keep plastic bags and film out of the bin — they jam the sorters
plastic.future_good: Rinsed and sorted by type, this bottle is flaked, washed, and melted into pellets — the raw material for a new bottle or even a fleece jacket.
plastic.future_bad: In the environment it never truly disappears. It fragments into microplastics that end up in soil, water, and the food chain, effectively forever.
plastic.rewrite: This one won't become a microplastic in the ocean. Sorted correctly, its polymer gets pulled back into the loop instead of drifting for centuries.
plastic.did_you_know: A plastic bottle recycled into fiber can end up as the fleece in a winter jacket.

trash.name: General waste
trash.tagline: Mixed or soiled items with no clean recycling stream.
trash.material: Mixed materials, food-soiled items, or things no local stream accepts.
trash.recyclable: Usually no — but worth a second look before it's written off.
trash.decompose_landfill: Varies enormously depending on what it's made of.
prep: Double-check: could it be rinsed and recycled instead?
Keep it out of the recycling bins — one soiled item can contaminate a whole batch
Reduce and reuse first; landfill is the last resort
second_life: Some general waste is burned for energy recovery
The best 'second life' here is buying less that ends up in this bin
trash.future_good: Kept out of the recycling stream, this item doesn't contaminate a whole batch of good material. Sometimes the most responsible move is an honest 'this one can't be recycled.'
trash.future_bad: Wish-cycled into the recycling bin, one greasy or mixed item can spoil everything around it, sending an entire batch to landfill instead of just itself.
trash.rewrite: Not everything can be reborn — and knowing the difference is its own kind of care. The real rewrite is choosing less of this next time.
trash.did_you_know: 'Wish-cycling' — hopefully tossing non-recyclables in the bin — is a leading cause of whole batches being rejected.

spotlights.title: The bottle that became a jacket
spotlights.body: Five clear plastic bottles hold enough polyester fiber to make the fill for one winter jacket. The bottle in your hand isn't waste — it's a raw material that hasn't been sorted yet.

spotlights.title: The jar that never dies
spotlights.body: Glass can be melted and reformed forever with no loss of quality. The jar you recycle today could still be circulating as a bottle a hundred years from now.

spotlights.title: The fastest recycler in your bin
spotlights.body: An aluminum can you recycle today can be melted, rolled, refilled, and back on a shelf in about 60 days — using a twentieth of the energy of making it new.

spotlights.title: Seven lives of a fiber
spotlights.body: The cardboard box at your door has likely been a box before. Its fibers can be pulped and pressed into new board five to seven times before they wear out.

spotlights.title: Seventeen trees a tonne
spotlights.body: Every tonne of paper you recycle keeps around 17 trees standing and saves thousands of liters of water compared with starting fresh.
